ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for economic beehoon with chicken cutlet and egg; mapped to rice vermicelli dish as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodle-based dish — stir-fried rice vermicelli with protein and egg
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean economic beehoon with chicken cutlet; A00LC (rice noodle dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Singaporean hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of economic beehoon with chicken cutlet and egg weighs approximately 300 g, comprising a base of stir-fried rice vermicelli (~180 g), one piece of deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet (~80 g), and one egg (~40 g).

nutrient_source.title: Economic Beehoon with Chicken Cutlet and Egg — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Economic Beehoon with Chicken Cutlet and Egg (cr_id F05060050). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (boiled) — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for economic beehoon with chicken cutlet and egg as a composite dish. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~58, medium category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate component. The breaded chicken cutlet and egg contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (58) × available carbohydrate per serving (36.8 g from 300 g × 19.45 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 21.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 469 mg/100g (1,407 mg per 300 g serving) exceeds 58% of the 2,400 mg daily sodium limit in a single meal, with minimal potassium or dietary fibre to offset the pressor effect.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, request reduced soy sauce seasoning, and substitute the deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet with grilled or steamed chicken to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a medium GI of approximately 58 but the low dietary fibre content (0.56 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate from the breaded chicken cutlet result in a high glycemic load of 21.4 per standard serving, posing significant postprandial glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to half a plate (~150 g), request grilled or steamed chicken without breading to reduce refined carbohydrate load, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: The deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet contributes saturated fat and trans fat precursors from frying oil, combined with high sodium (469 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.56 g/100g), creating an unfavourable cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the deep-fried chicken cutlet and request a grilled or steamed protein alternative, reduce portion size to 150 g, and avoid adding soy sauce or other high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation, and sodium at 469 mg/100g exceeds the recommended threshold for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried chicken cutlet due to AGE formation, request grilled or steamed chicken, and limit portion size to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Beehoon with Chicken Cutlet and Egg is a popular Singaporean hawker-centre and school-canteen dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (beehoon) served alongside a deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet and a cooked egg. The rice vermicelli is wok-tossed with soy sauce, light seasoning, and vegetable oil, producing a mildly savoury, slightly chewy noodle base. The chicken cutlet is coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy, contributing a crunchy texture and rich umami flavour. The egg is typically fried or hard-boiled. Per 100 g, the dish provides 205 kcal, 10.34 g protein, 9.51 g fat, and 19.45 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 469 mg — exceeding the 400 mg/100g threshold relevant to hypertension management. Dietary fibre is low at 0.56 g/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the rice vermicelli component, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 21.4 per standard 300 g serving. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification — primarily substituting the deep-fried breaded cutlet with grilled or steamed chicken and reducing soy sauce. It is a halal-certified dish commonly consumed as a working lunch or school meal in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05060050) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the rice vermicelli analogue, which is the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100g based on typical composition of deep-fried breaded chicken cutlet and stir-fried rice vermicelli from USDA FoodData Central analogues; HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this composite dish, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates. Cultural note: the input record listed primary_cuisine as 'western' but this dish is a Singaporean hawker dish; corrected to 'Singaporean'. Halal dietary tag preserved from input.
